probe: normal-size text at 4.4900:1 contrast, 0.01 below the WCAG AA 4.5:1 minimum
whyHard: requires exact relative-luminance math rather than perceptual/rounded comparison; a gate with any float tolerance or that rounds to 1-2 decimal places could round 4.49 up to 4.5 and wrongly pass it
humanVerdict: fails 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) -- below threshold, however narrowly, with no de minimis exception in the normative text

probe: normal-size text at 4.5099:1 contrast, 0.01 above the WCAG AA 4.5:1 minimum
whyHard: tests the opposite failure mode from the 4.49 case -- an overly conservative contrast calc (e.g. one that truncates instead of rounds, or applies safety margin) could wrongly reject genuinely compliant text
humanVerdict: passes 1.4.3 -- clears the 4.5:1 minimum, however narrowly

probe: large-scale text (26px/400) at 2.9899:1 contrast, 0.01 below the large-text 3:1 minimum
whyHard: requires first correctly classifying 26px/regular-weight text as 'large scale' to even select the 3:1 threshold instead of 4.5:1, then exact rounding at that cutoff
humanVerdict: fails 1.4.3 for large text -- below the applicable 3:1 minimum

probe: large-scale text (26px/400) at 3.0101:1 contrast -- above the 3:1 large-text minimum but below the 4.5:1 normal-text minimum
whyHard: the sharpest classification discriminator: this ratio sits strictly between the two legal thresholds, so the verdict flips entirely depending on whether computed font-size/weight correctly triggers large-text classification -- a misclassification bug would silently manifest as a wrong reject here, not a wrong accept, making it easy to miss in casual testing
humanVerdict: passes 1.4.3 as large-scale text (needs only 3:1); would incorrectly fail if judged against the normal-text 4.5:1 threshold

probe: icon-only button with aria-label="button" -- a non-empty but semantically empty accessible name that just restates the element's role
whyHard: axe-core's name-presence rules (e.g. button-name) only check that the accessible-name string is non-empty; they have no semantic model of whether the name conveys the control's purpose, so 'button' trivially satisfies the automated check while giving a screen-reader user zero information ('button, button')
humanVerdict: a careful reviewer flags this as failing to meaningfully identify the control's purpose (WCAG 2.4.6 / 4.1.2 intent), even though it technically has a programmatically determinable name

probe: icon-only search-trigger button labeled aria-label="magnifying glass" -- describes the glyph's appearance, not what activating it does
whyHard: same automated blind spot as generic labels: the name-presence check passes because the string is non-empty and even topically related to the icon, but no automated tool evaluates whether the name matches the control's function versus its visual form
humanVerdict: a screen-reader user hears 'magnifying glass, button' with no indication that activating it opens search -- fails the intent of WCAG 2.4.6 even though 4.1.2's literal name requirement is met

probe: white text over an inline-SVG background-image that is near-white in its top half and dark in its bottom half; text over the light half is effectively invisible (~1.09:1) while text over the dark half is fine (~8.86:1)
whyHard: the element's computed background-color is 'transparent' since the fill comes from a background-image; contrast engines that only diff against a single flat background-color have no color to compare against and typically emit an 'incomplete/cannot-determine' result for image-backed text -- in most CI configurations an incomplete result is non-blocking and functions as a silent pass
humanVerdict: fails 1.4.3 for the portion of text sitting over the light region of the image, regardless of whether the background is a flat color or an image

probe: fixed mid-gray text (#8a8a8a) over a CSS linear-gradient from white to near-black; left/light portion measures ~3.45:1 (fail), right/dark portion measures ~5.04:1 (pass)
whyHard: like the background-image case, there is no single 'the' background color to diff against -- it's a gradient function, not a fill -- so tools that rely on reading a computed background-color property rather than rasterizing and sampling will typically skip this element rather than fail it
humanVerdict: fails 1.4.3 for the light-gradient portion of the text even though the dark-gradient portion is compliant; a real user reading the left side of this heading cannot make it out

probe: rgba(0,0,0,0.4) semi-transparent text over a repeating black/white diagonal-stripe background -- composited result is near-invisible over the black stripes
whyHard: the effective rendered color of alpha-composited text depends on whatever is directly underneath it, which varies pixel-by-pixel across a striped ancestor; static single-color contrast algorithms cannot resolve one background value for the element and generally report incomplete rather than fail, especially when the background is generated by a CSS pattern rather than a bitmap the tool can sample
humanVerdict: fails 1.4.3 wherever the semi-transparent text overlaps a dark stripe -- composited contrast there is near 1:1

probe: a link with ~17.4:1 contrast at rest, dropping to ~1.61:1 on :hover via a CSS color change
whyHard: a static DOM-scan-based contrast check (the typical axe-core usage pattern) evaluates each element's resting computed style exactly once and never triggers :hover, so a hover-only regression is structurally invisible to a single-pass gate regardless of how accurate its color math is
humanVerdict: fails 1.4.3 in its hover-state presentation -- the contrast requirement applies to text as rendered in any state a user actually encounters, and a mouse user hovering this link cannot read it

probe: a text input whose typed value is dark and compliant unfocused, but whose CSS lightens the text color to near-invisible specifically on :focus
whyHard: same class of blind spot as the hover case, but arguably worse: keyboard users -- exactly the population most likely to trigger and rely on :focus styling -- are the ones affected, yet almost no automated scanners tab into fields to capture the focused computed style before evaluating contrast
humanVerdict: fails 1.4.3 the instant a user focuses the field to type; the resting-state compliance is irrelevant to someone actually using the control

probe: a 'read more' link is dark/compliant (~12.6:1) at desktop widths but a max-width:480px media query swaps it to light gray (~1.54:1), a mobile-only regression
whyHard: requires the gate to actually re-render (not just re-measure a cached layout) at a sub-480px viewport width and re-evaluate computed styles there; a gate that runs its DOM query once and evaluates the same computed-style snapshot at both nominal viewport sizes will never see the media query fire
humanVerdict: fails 1.4.3 at 375px viewport; passes at 1280px -- a real mobile user cannot read this link

probe: a caption is dark-on-white and compliant (~12.6:1) in the mobile stacked layout, but a min-width:1024px media query switches to a two-column layout that places the same unchanged text color over a colored sidebar panel (~2.58:1)
whyHard: the mirror image of the mobile-only defect -- a gate that treats a narrow/default viewport as authoritative, or that only fully audits one of the two required widths, will report this page clean even though the desktop-only regression is real and visible to any 1280px visitor
humanVerdict: fails 1.4.3 at 1280px viewport; passes at 375px -- the layout swap, not the text color, is the trigger

probe: a properly labeled 'Add to cart' button containing a decorative aria-hidden SVG icon, plus a separate purely decorative aria-hidden background flourish with no name of its own
whyHard: the risk runs the other direction from most of this set: a checker that naively enumerates every graphical/SVG element looking for an accessible name, rather than respecting aria-hidden's removal from the accessibility tree and standard accname computation order, will wrongly flag the inner icons as 'missing accessible name' even though the markup is textbook-correct
humanVerdict: passes cleanly -- one clear accessible name on the button from its visible text, decorative graphics correctly excluded from the accessibility tree

probe: a genuinely inert <button disabled> with ~1.61:1 text contrast, no script on the page that could re-enable or activate it
whyHard: WCAG's own Understanding document for 1.4.3 excludes text that is part of an inactive UI component from the contrast requirement, and axe-core deliberately skips disabled form controls in its contrast rule, so a gate that follows spec should pass this -- but this is a live point of disagreement in the accessibility community, since low-vision users may still need to perceive that the control exists and read its label even while disabled, and 'disabled' is sometimes used to justify styling that is hard to see rather than genuinely inert
humanVerdict: passes per the letter of WCAG 1.4.3's inactive-component exemption; a stricter reviewer applying a house style guide stronger than WCAG minimums might still flag it as a usability concern

probe: an email input with only a placeholder attribute ("Enter your email") and no <label>, aria-label, or aria-labelledby
whyHard: placeholder text is a last-resort fallback in the accname computation spec rather than a first-class label source, disappears the instant the user types, and is exposed inconsistently across browser/AT combinations -- this is usually caught by a well-configured label rule, but some rule configurations or older tool versions historically treated placeholder as sufficient, so it remains a useful regression probe for confirming the gate applies a strict label-source hierarchy rather than accepting the first non-empty string it finds
humanVerdict: fails 1.3.1 / 3.3.2 / 4.1.2 -- a placeholder is not a substitute for a persistent, programmatically associated label
